output:
(B).

Each failure is an instance of the same structural pattern: **a foundational assumption was hardcoded as a fixed axiom rather than modeled as a variable with its own failure boundary.**

- The risk model didn't parameterize "housing prices can decline nationally" — it excluded that state from the possibility space entirely.
- The flood model didn't parameterize "the rainfall distribution itself can shift" — it treated the historical distribution as stationary.
- The pandemic model didn't parameterize "transmission can occur before symptoms" — it inherited the influenza transmission structure as a given.

In all three cases the model was internally valid. The failure wasn't calibration (A), data volume (C), or generic overconfidence (D). It was that **the boundary condition under which the model stops being valid was itself invisible to the model**. None included a mechanism to detect when reality had crossed outside their assumption envelope.

This is the difference between a model being *wrong* and a model being *unaware of the conditions under which it becomes wrong*.
